symptoms.symptom_name: Postural lightheadedness / presyncope relieved immediately by lying flat
symptoms.onset: within a few minutes of standing

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Strictly postural cerebral hypoperfusion symptoms with immediate supine relief are characteristic of orthostatic intolerance.
Postural presyncope is shared, but is distinguished by the blood-pressure response.

symptoms.symptom_name: Excessive sustained sinus tachycardia on standing (approx 75 supine to 125-140 standing)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A sustained HR rise of >=30 bpm within 10 minutes of standing without hypotension is the defining feature of POTS.
IST tachycardia is present at rest and non-postural; a purely posture-triggered rise argues against it.
A gradual sinus rise tied to posture is less typical of an abrupt-onset re-entrant SVT, but does not exclude it without an ECG.

symptoms.symptom_name: Dependent acrocyanosis of the lower legs and feet on standing, resolving supine

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Gravitational venous pooling producing reversible dependent acrocyanosis is a recognised and fairly specific feature of POTS.
An objective, posture-dependent physical colour change is not a feature of anxiety.
Sympathetic activation on standing produces tremor and jitteriness, consistent with the hyperadrenergic response in POTS.
Tremor and jitteriness overlap with anxiety, which is why this symptom is a decoy toward the anchoring trap.

symptoms.symptom_name: Chronic fatigue, exercise intolerance and brain fog

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Fatigue, exercise intolerance and cognitive 'brain fog' are core chronic features of the POTS symptom complex.

symptoms.symptom_name: No true syncope or fall (presyncope only)

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of true syncope lowers (but does not eliminate) the probability of a dangerous arrhythmic cause.
Presyncope without frank syncope is the usual pattern in POTS.

symptoms.symptom_name: Tachycardia abolished by lying flat; absent at rest and in sleep

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A tachycardia that is entirely posture-dependent and abolished supine is characteristic of POTS.
A primary tachyarrhythmia would not be reliably abolished simply by lying down.
IST persists at rest; abolition on lying flat argues against it.

symptoms.symptom_name: No chest pain; attempting heavy oral fluid intake

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: Absence of chest pain modestly lowers concern for an acute cardiac event, though it does not exclude arrhythmia.

symptoms.symptom_name: Blood pressure maintained (or slightly rising) on standing — no orthostatic hypotension

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A stable BP on standing (supine ~110/70, standing ~115/75) with a marked HR rise defines the POTS haemodynamic pattern and excludes orthostatic hypotension — but this is a clinician-measured active stand, sealed from telehealth.
No sustained fall in systolic BP on standing rules out true orthostatic hypotension.

symptoms.symptom_name: Sustained HR increase of ~50 bpm within 10 minutes of standing on clinician active stand testing

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A clinician-confirmed sustained HR rise >=30 bpm within 10 minutes of standing without hypotension is the diagnostic haemodynamic criterion — obtainable only by an in-person active stand or tilt, hence sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: Structurally normal heart, regular rhythm, no murmur on examination

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: A structurally normal heart with regular rhythm supports a functional dysautonomia rather than structural heart disease — but auscultation is clinician-only and sealed.

symptoms.symptom_name: 12-lead ECG / rhythm strip to exclude a paroxysmal tachyarrhythmia

investigation_details.expected_result: Sinus rhythm with posture-related sinus tachycardia and no arrhythmia — expected but not yet obtained
investigation_details.if_not_ordered_consequence: A paroxysmal supraventricular tachyarrhythmia could be missed and misattributed to dysautonomia.

diagnostic_weight_edges.evidence_basis: An ECG/rhythm strip is required to exclude a primary tachyarrhythmia before a dysautonomia is confirmed.

symptom_clusters.clinical_significance: Postural presyncope, excessive sustained postural sinus tachycardia, dependent acrocyanosis and a confirmed >=30 bpm active-stand HR rise together, with maintained blood pressure, are highly specific for POTS.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Active stand test / head-up tilt with paired supine and standing BP and HR
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: The defining haemodynamic test for POTS and the discriminator from orthostatic hypotension; requires in-person measurement.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: 12-lead ECG (plus ambulatory monitoring if diagnostic doubt)
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: To exclude a paroxysmal tachyarrhythmia before attributing symptoms to dysautonomia.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Basic bloods (FBC, electrolytes, TSH, ferritin) and consideration of morning cortisol
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: To screen for anaemia, thyrotoxicosis, electrolyte disturbance and other secondary drivers of tachycardia — outpatient, not emergent.

investigation_recommendations_expected.investigation_name: Echocardiography as a routine first-line test
investigation_recommendations_expected.rationale: Not required first-line in a structurally normal heart with a clear postural pattern; reserve for specialist direction to avoid over-investigation.
